items.q: Are the hook and the chorus the same thing?
items.correct: No - the hook is a short catchy idea that often lives in the chorus but can also be a riff, a lyric or a rhythm anywhere in the song.
items.misconception: Hook is simply another word for the chorus.
items.random_wrong: The hook is the cable that connects the guitar to the amplifier.
items.why: A chorus is a section; a hook is an idea. Many songs have instrumental hooks in the intro, so equating the two terms causes analysis errors.

items.q: Are sampling and remixing the same technique?
items.correct: No - sampling reuses an extract of an existing recording inside a new track, while remixing reworks the elements of one existing track into a new version.
items.misconception: Sampling and remixing both just mean changing a song a bit.
items.random_wrong: Sampling means performing a song live; remixing means recording it.
items.why: Both reuse recorded material, which is why students blur them, but the source relationship differs: a sample is an ingredient, a remix is a rebuild.

items.q: Is a DAW a kind of synthesiser?
items.correct: No - a DAW is software for recording, editing, arranging and mixing audio and MIDI; synthesisers are instruments that can run inside it.
items.misconception: A DAW is just a digital keyboard that makes sounds.
items.random_wrong: A DAW is a special pair of studio headphones.
items.why: The DAW is the studio environment, not the sound source; distinguishing tools from instruments is needed to describe modern production accurately.
Correct answer: I-IV-V. “I-ii-iii” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Reusing audio in a new track is called?
items.why: It's Sampling. “Mixing” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is the function of the bridge in a pop song?
items.correct: To provide contrast before the final chorus, often by changing melody, key, or texture.
items.misconception: It is the same as the chorus.
items.random_wrong: It is the very first section.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: To provide contrast before the final chorus, often by changing melody, key, or texture.. “It is the same as the chorus.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How does D Dorian differ from D minor?
items.correct: Dorian has a raised 6th (B natural) compared to natural minor.
items.random_wrong: It is the very first section.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Dorian has a raised 6th (B natural) compared to natural minor.. “They are the same scale.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: A recurring main theme (A) alternates with contrasting episodes (B, C, etc.).
items.misconception: Dorian has no sharps or flats at all.
items.random_wrong: A round where voices enter at different times.
items.why: Correct answer: A recurring main theme (A) alternates with contrasting episodes (B, C, etc.).. “Dorian has no sharps or flats at all.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of these is the best definition of “Hook”?
items.correct: Catchy musical phrase that grabs the listener; often part of the chorus.
items.misconception: Common pop structure alternating verses and a memorable repeated chorus.
items.random_wrong: Short, repeated melodic or rhythmic idea, common in rock and jazz.
items.why: It's Catchy musical phrase that grabs the listener; often part of the chorus.. “Common pop structure alternating verses and a memorable repeated chorus.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Common pop structure alternating verses and a memorable repeated chorus.
items.misconception: Catchy musical phrase that grabs the listener; often part of the chorus.

items.q: Which of these is the best definition of “Sampling”?
items.correct: Reusing a portion of a sound recording in a new track, central to hip-hop.
items.misconception: Short, repeated melodic or rhythmic idea, common in rock and jazz.
items.random_wrong: Catchy musical phrase that grabs the listener; often part of the chorus.
items.why: Correct answer: Reusing a portion of a sound recording in a new track, central to hip-hop.. “Short, repeated melodic or rhythmic idea, common in rock and jazz.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many bars in a standard blues chorus?
items.why: Correct answer: 12 bars. “11 bars” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
